tier_1_regimens.site: Solitary plasmacytoma or solitary plasmacytoma with minimal marrow involvement
tier_1_regimens.setting: Primary
tier_1_regimens.notes: Radiation therapy is the intervention of choice for solitary plasmacytoma. Recommended RT dose: 40–50 Gy in 1.8–2.0 Gy fractions (20–25 total fractions) to the involved site. For solitary plasmacytomas <5 cm in size, treatment with 35–40 Gy is an acceptable alternative due to high local control rates. Consider surgery if structurally unstable or if there is neurologic compromise due to mass effect. Reassess after at least 3 months following radiation as assessment of response with imaging may not be accurate sooner. All plasmacytomas should be imaged yearly, preferably with the same technique used at diagnosis, for at least 5 years.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Primary
tier_1_regimens.notes: Daratumumab monotherapy improved 5-year PFS compared to active monitoring in patients with high-risk smoldering myeloma. In subgroup analysis by 20/2/20 criteria, the PFS benefit was clear in patients with high-risk SMM (HR, 0.36; 95% CI, 0.23–0.58) but did not reach statistical significance in those with intermediate-risk or low-risk SMM.

tier_2_handbook.definition: Multiple myeloma (MM) is a malignant neoplasm of plasma cells that typically accumulate in the bone marrow, leading to end-organ damage and monoclonal gammopathy. The diagnosis of symptomatic (active) MM requires either clonal bone marrow plasma cells (BMPCs) ≥10% or biopsy-proven bony or extramedullary plasmacytoma, along with at least one myeloma-defining event (MDE). MDEs include the classic CRAB criteria: hypercalcemia (serum calcium >0.25 mmol/L above the upper limit of normal or >2.75 mmol/L), renal insufficiency (creatinine clearance <40 mL/min or serum creatinine >177 μmol/L), anemia (hemoglobin >20 g/L below the lower limit of normal or <100 g/L), and bone lesions (one or more osteolytic lesions on skeletal radiography, CT, or PET-CT). Alternatively, MDEs can be biomarkers of malignancy: clonal BMPCs ≥60%, involved:uninvolved serum free light chain ratio (FLCr) ≥100, or >1 focal lesion on MRI. Smoldering (asymptomatic) MM is defined by the presence of serum monoclonal protein (M-protein) ≥3 g/dL and/or clonal BMPCs 10%–59% in the absence of MDEs or amyloidosis. Solitary plasmacytoma is a biopsy-proven solitary lesion of bone or soft tissue with clonal plasma cells, normal skeletal survey and MRI of the spine and pelvis (except for the primary lesion), absence of MDEs, and normal bone marrow with no clonal plasma cells. Solitary plasmacytoma with minimal marrow involvement has similar criteria but with clonal BMPCs <10%. Plasma cell leukemia is defined by the presence of ≥5% plasma cells in the peripheral blood. MM is a disease of the bone marrow, and while it originates from B-cell lineage, its embryological origin is not a typical descriptor in clinical guidelines.

subtypes.name: Smoldering (Asymptomatic) Myeloma
subtypes.description: A precursor state characterized by M-protein ≥3 g/dL and/or clonal BMPCs 10%–59% without evidence of end-organ damage or MDEs. It carries a risk of progression to active MM.
subtypes.frequency: Represents approximately 10% of MM cases at diagnosis.
subtypes.key_associations: Risk of progression is stratified by the Mayo 20/2/20 model (BMPCs >20%, M-protein >2 g/dL, FLCr >20). Two or more of these factors confer high risk.

subtypes.name: Active (Symptomatic) Multiple Myeloma
subtypes.description: Defined by clonal BMPCs ≥10% or plasmacytoma plus at least one MDE (CRAB criteria or SLiM biomarkers).

subtypes.name: Solitary Plasmacytoma
subtypes.description: A localized clonal plasma cell neoplasm involving a single bone or soft tissue site, without systemic MM criteria.
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for approximately 3-5% of all plasma cell neoplasms.
subtypes.key_associations: High risk of progression to systemic MM (14%–38% within 3 years). Treated primarily with radiation therapy.

subtypes.name: Plasma Cell Leukemia
subtypes.description: An aggressive variant defined by the presence of ≥5% clonal plasma cells in the peripheral blood.
subtypes.key_associations: Associated with high tumor burden, extramedullary disease, and poor prognosis.

subtypes.name: High-Risk Molecular/Cytogenetic Subtypes
subtypes.description: Subtypes defined by specific chromosomal abnormalities detected by FISH, which confer adverse prognosis.
subtypes.key_associations: Includes del(17p), t(4;14), t(14;16), t(14;20), 1q21 gain/amplification, and 1p deletion. These are incorporated into staging systems (R-ISS, R2-ISS, IMS-IMWG).

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The molecular pathogenesis of multiple myeloma (MM) is complex and characterized by several key genomic events. The disease is initiated by the malignant transformation of a B-cell or early plasma cell in the germinal center. A hallmark is the translocation of the immunoglobulin heavy chain (IGH) gene locus at 14q32, which juxtaposes oncogenes under the control of strong enhancer elements. The most common IGH translocations are t(11;14)(q13;q32), t(4;14)(p16;q32), t(14;16)(q32;q23), and t(14;20)(q32;q12). Deletion of the short arm of chromosome 17 (del(17p13)) is a high-risk abnormality, as it encompasses the TP53 tumor suppressor gene. Mutation of TP53 is also a poor prognostic marker and requires DNA sequencing for detection. Abnormalities of chromosome 1 are frequent: gain or amplification of 1q21 is associated with disease progression and poorer outcomes, with a higher incidence in relapsed MM. Deletion of 1p32 is another high-risk cytogenetic feature. Other recurrent abnormalities include deletions of 13q and hyperdiploidy. Risk stratification based on these chromosomal aberrations, as defined by FISH on CD138-positive purified plasma cells, is critical for prognostic counseling and treatment selection. The R-ISS and the newer IMS-IMWG staging systems integrate these genetic findings with clinical markers like beta-2 microglobulin and LDH to define standard and high-risk disease. Gene expression profiling can also identify high-risk molecular signatures.

epidemiology.incidence: Multiple myeloma (MM) accounts for approximately 1.8% of all cancers and 17% of hematologic malignancies. The American Cancer Society estimated 36,100 new MM cases in the United States in 2025. The age-adjusted incidence rate is approximately 6.6 per 100,000 per year. Globally, the age-standardized prevalence and incidence have increased between 1990 and 2021.
epidemiology.mortality: It is estimated that there were 12,030 deaths from MM in the United States in 2025. The 5-year relative survival rate is approximately 55%.
epidemiology.trends: Incidence and mortality rates have been rising slightly over the past two decades, likely due to population aging and improved diagnostics. However, survival has also improved significantly with the advent of novel therapies.

demographics.age: The median age at diagnosis is 69 years, with most cases diagnosed between the ages of 65 and 74. MM is uncommon in individuals under 40.
demographics.sex: The disease is slightly more common in males than females, with a male-to-female ratio of approximately 1.4:1.
demographics.race_ethnicity: There are significant racial and ethnic disparities. Black/African American individuals have the highest incidence rate, approximately twice that of White individuals, and are diagnosed at a younger age. They also present with more aggressive disease features, including increased anemia, hypercalcemia, renal dysfunction, and extramedullary disease at diagnosis. The global burden, including cases, deaths, and disability-adjusted life years, is higher in males and has increased from 1990 to 2021.

risk_factors.detail: Risk increases substantially with age, with the majority of cases diagnosed after age 65.
risk_factors.strength: Strong. The median age of diagnosis is 69 years.

risk_factors.detail: Black/African American individuals have the highest incidence rate of any racial/ethnic group, approximately twice that of other groups, and present with more severe symptoms and higher-risk disease biology.
MGUS is a precursor state with a risk of progression to MM of approximately 1% per year. The presence of MGUS confers a 25-fold increased risk of developing MM.
risk_factors.strength: Very strong. MGUS precedes the vast majority of MM cases.

risk_factors.detail: A higher-risk precursor than MGUS. Risk of progression to active MM varies by risk stratification: low risk (0 factors per 20/2/20 model) has a ~2-year progression risk of 6%, while high risk (2-3 factors) has a ~44% 2-year risk.
risk_factors.strength: Strong. Direct precursor to active MM.

risk_factors.detail: First-degree relatives of patients with MM have a 2- to 4-fold increased risk of developing MM or related plasma cell disorders.
Elevated body mass index (BMI ≥25) has been associated with an increased risk of developing MM.
risk_factors.strength: Moderate. Cited in the IMPEDE score for VTE risk.

risk_factors.detail: Specific chromosomal abnormalities (e.g., del(17p), t(4;14), 1q gain) are not causal but are high-risk prognostic factors associated with more aggressive disease and poorer outcomes.
risk_factors.strength: Strong for prognostication, not for initial risk.

risk_factors.detail: Use of immunomodulatory drugs (IMiDs like lenalidomide, thalidomide), high-dose dexamethasone, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, and multiagent chemotherapy significantly increases the risk of venous thromboembolism (VTE), especially in the first 6 months after diagnosis.

protective_factors: The NCCN guidelines do not explicitly list established protective factors for the development of multiple myeloma. However, in the discussion of disparities, it is noted that Black/African American individuals have a superior median overall survival compared to White individuals despite higher incidence, which may be attributed to biological differences or differential response to therapy. This is an observation of outcome rather than a protective factor against disease development.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Multiple myeloma (MM) is a malignant neoplasm of plasma cells that typically accumulates in the bone marrow, leading to bone destruction, elevated blood calcium, and anemia, as well as renal damage from secreted monoclonal protein (M-protein). It is most frequently diagnosed in people aged 65 to 74 years, with a median age of 69 years. The American Cancer Society estimated 36,100 new cases and 12,030 deaths in the United States in 2025 [2]. Globally, age-standardized prevalence, mortality, and disability-adjusted life years have increased from 1990 to 2021, with higher burden in males [3]. Patients often present with symptoms related to organ damage from M-protein or plasma cell infiltration, such as bone pain, fatigue, weakness, weight loss, nausea, constipation, and confusion.

symptoms.name: Bone pain
symptoms.detail: A common symptom due to osteolytic lesions or fractures. Pain may be localized or diffuse.
symptoms.alarm: Severe or new-onset pain may indicate pathological fracture or spinal cord compression.

symptoms.name: Fatigue and weakness
symptoms.detail: Often due to anemia or renal dysfunction.
symptoms.alarm: Rapidly worsening fatigue may signal disease progression or complications like infection.

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: May occur due to metabolic changes or decreased oral intake.
symptoms.alarm: Unexplained weight loss could indicate advanced disease or other comorbidities.

symptoms.name: Nausea and constipation
symptoms.detail: Can result from hypercalcemia or renal insufficiency.
symptoms.alarm: Persistent symptoms may indicate severe hypercalcemia or renal failure.

symptoms.name: Confusion
symptoms.detail: May be due to hypercalcemia, uremia, or hyperviscosity.
symptoms.alarm: Acute confusion requires urgent evaluation for metabolic or neurological emergencies.

symptoms.name: Recurrent infections
symptoms.detail: Due to immune dysfunction from abnormal plasma cells and treatment.
symptoms.alarm: Severe infections (e.g., pneumonia, sepsis) are medical emergencies.

signs.name: Bone tenderness
signs.detail: On palpation, indicative of lytic lesions or fractures.

signs.name: Anemia
signs.detail: Hemoglobin <10 g/dL or >2 g/dL below normal, due to bone marrow involvement.

signs.name: Hypercalcemia
signs.detail: Serum calcium >0.25 mmol/L (>1 mg/dL) above upper limit of normal or >2.75 mmol/L (>11 mg/dL).

signs.name: Renal insufficiency
signs.detail: Creatinine clearance <40 mL/min or serum creatinine >177 μmol/L (>2 mg/dL).

signs.name: Bone lesions
signs.detail: Osteolytic lesions on imaging (skeletal survey, CT, or PET-CT).

signs.name: Hyperviscosity symptoms
signs.detail: Visual changes, headache, or bleeding due to high M-protein levels.

atypical_or_missed: Asymptomatic presentation in smoldering myeloma, which may delay diagnosis.
Presentation with isolated extramedullary plasmacytoma without systemic symptoms.
Neurological symptoms (e.g., peripheral neuropathy) without classic CRAB features.
Recurrent infections as the initial manifestation, which may be mistaken for other immune disorders.
Renal dysfunction without prominent bone disease, leading to misdiagnosis as primary kidney disease.

red_flags: Severe bone pain or new neurological deficits suggesting spinal cord compression.
Acute kidney injury with serum creatinine >2 mg/dL or rapid decline in renal function.
Hypercalcemia with serum calcium >11 mg/dL causing confusion or arrhythmias.
Anemia with hemoglobin <8 g/dL or symptomatic anemia (e.g., dyspnea, chest pain).
Recurrent or severe infections (e.g., pneumonia, sepsis) due to immune dysfunction.
Hyperviscosity symptoms (visual changes, headache, altered mental status) requiring urgent plasmapheresis.
Pathological fractures or imminent fracture risk from lytic lesions.
Plasma cell leukemia (≥5% circulating plasma cells) indicating aggressive disease.

diagnostic.test: Complete blood count (CBC) with differential and platelet count
diagnostic.rationale: Assess for anemia, thrombocytopenia, or leukopenia due to bone marrow infiltration.
Look for rouleaux formation, myeloma cells, or other abnormalities.

diagnostic.test: Serum chemistry (BUN, creatinine, electrolytes, calcium, albumin, LDH, uric acid)
diagnostic.rationale: Evaluate for renal dysfunction, hypercalcemia, and other metabolic abnormalities.
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated creatinine, hypercalcemia, low albumin, elevated LDH.

diagnostic.rationale: Prognostic marker and used in staging (ISS).
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated levels (>3.5 mg/L in ISS stage I, >5.5 mg/L in stage III).

diagnostic.rationale: Detect monoclonal gammopathy and quantify M-protein.

diagnostic.test: Serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP) and serum immunofixation electrophoresis (SIFE)
diagnostic.rationale: Identify and quantify M-protein; immunofixation determines type.
diagnostic.key_findings: M-spike on SPEP, positive immunofixation for monoclonal protein.

diagnostic.test: 24-hour urine for total protein, urine protein electrophoresis (UPEP), and urine immunofixation electrophoresis (UIFE)
diagnostic.rationale: Detect Bence Jones proteinuria and quantify urinary M-protein.
diagnostic.key_findings: Increased urinary protein, M-protein on UPEP, positive UIFE.

diagnostic.rationale: Assess light chain involvement, prognostication, and response monitoring.
diagnostic.key_findings: Abnormal FLC ratio (<0.26 or >1.65), involved FLC >100 mg/L.

diagnostic.test: Unilateral bone marrow aspirate and biopsy with immunohistochemistry (IHC) and/or multi-parameter flow cytometry
diagnostic.rationale: Confirm clonal plasma cells, quantify bone marrow involvement, and assess phenotype.

diagnostic.test: Plasma cell FISH panel (CD138-positive selected sample)
diagnostic.rationale: Detect cytogenetic abnormalities for risk stratification.
diagnostic.key_findings: del(17p), t(4;14), t(11;14), t(14;16), t(14;20), 1q21 gain/amplification, 1p deletion.

diagnostic.rationale: Identify TP53 mutation, a high-risk feature.

staging.test: Whole-body FDG-PET/CT (preferred) or whole-body low-dose CT
staging.rationale: Detect osteolytic lesions and extramedullary disease; superior to skeletal survey.
staging.indication: Initial diagnostic workup for all patients with suspected MM or plasmacytoma.

staging.rationale: Evaluate bone marrow infiltration, especially if FDG-PET/CT or CT is negative; discern smoldering from active MM.
staging.indication: Consider if FDG-PET/CT or low-dose CT is negative.

staging.rationale: Traditional imaging but less sensitive; acceptable if advanced imaging unavailable.
staging.indication: Use in certain circumstances when FDG-PET/CT or low-dose CT not available.

staging.rationale: Evaluate for cardiac involvement, especially if amyloidosis suspected.
staging.indication: Consider if clinical suspicion for amyloidosis or heart failure.

biomarkers.rationale: Assess for cardiac amyloidosis or heart failure.
biomarkers.when: Consider if symptoms of heart failure or suspected amyloidosis.
If clinical symptoms of hyperviscosity (visual changes, bleeding, neurological symptoms).

biomarkers.test: Hepatitis B and C testing and HIV screening
biomarkers.rationale: Screen for viral infections, especially before immunosuppressive therapy.

biomarkers.test: Clonotype identification or storage of bone marrow sample for MRD testing by NGS
Bone marrow biopsy for FISH, NGS, or flow cytometry
biomarkers.rationale: Assess for cytogenetic abnormalities and clonal plasma cells.
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis and as clinically indicated during follow-up.

biomarkers.rationale: Evaluate for cast nephropathy, amyloidosis, or other renal pathology.
biomarkers.when: If albuminuria or abnormal renal function without clear cause.

do_not_miss: Bone marrow biopsy with FISH panel for cytogenetics.
Serum and urine protein electrophoresis with immunofixation.
Whole-body FDG-PET/CT or low-dose CT for bone disease.
Assessment for renal dysfunction (creatinine clearance, urinalysis).
avoid: Relying solely on skeletal survey for bone disease assessment (insensitive).
Skipping bone marrow biopsy even if plasmacytoma biopsy confirms plasma cells (need cytogenetics).
Using DEXA scans to assess myeloma bone disease (not useful).
Delaying systemic therapy for advanced imaging if diagnosis is clear.

staging.system: International Staging System (ISS), Revised ISS (R-ISS), R2-ISS, and International Myeloma Society/International Myeloma Working Group (IMS-IMWG) staging.

t_stages.stage: Not defined by TNM; staging based on ISS/R-ISS criteria.

n_stages.stage: Not applicable; plasma cell disorders do not use nodal staging.

m_stages.stage: Not defined by TNM; disease is systemic.

stage_groups.criteria: Serum beta-2 microglobulin <3.5 mg/L and serum albumin ≥3.5 g/dL.
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Low tumor burden and better prognosis.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Median survival not reached in studies; ~80% at 5 years.

stage_groups.criteria: Not Stage I or III (beta-2 microglobulin ≥3.5 mg/L and <5.5 mg/L, or beta-2 microglobulin <3.5 mg/L and albumin <3.5 g/dL).

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: High tumor burden and poor prognosis.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative intent with therapy, but may consider clinical trials.

stage_groups.criteria: ISS stage I and standard-risk chromosomal abnormalities by FISH (no del(17p), t(4;14), or t(14;16)) and serum LDH ≤ upper limit of normal (ULN).
Not R-ISS stage I or III (includes most patients).
ISS stage III and either high-risk chromosomal abnormalities [del(17p), t(4;14), t(14;16)] by FISH or serum LDH > ULN.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Consider aggressive therapy and clinical trials.

stage_groups.criteria: Not ISS stage II or III, serum LDH ≤ ULN, del(17p)/t(4;14)/1q+ not detected.
ISS stage II or serum LDH > ULN or del(17p)/t(4;14)/1q+ detected.
Any combination of high-risk features (ISS stage, LDH, del(17p), t(4;14), 1q+).
Any combination of high-risk features totaling 3-5 points (ISS stage III, del(17p), t(4;14), 1q+, LDH > ULN).
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Clinical trials, aggressive therapy, consider allogeneic transplant.

stage_groups.criteria: All patients not meeting high-risk criteria.

Any of: del(17p) (>20% of plasma cells) and/or TP53 mutation; t(4;14), t(14;16), or t(14;20) co-occurring with 1q+ and/or del(1p32); monoallelic del(1p32) with 1q+ or biallelic del(1p32); high β2M (>5.5 mg/dL) with normal creatinine (<1.2 mg/dL).
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Consider aggressive therapy and clinical trials.

staging_pearls: Risk stratification evolves; new systems (R2-ISS, IMS-IMWG) incorporate cytogenetics and LDH for better prognostication.
High-risk cytogenetics (del(17p), t(4;14), t(14;16)) are independent poor prognostic factors.
1q21 gain/amplification is common and associated with high risk, especially if co-occurring with other abnormalities.
Plasma cell leukemia (≥5% circulating plasma cells) is a very high-risk feature.
Staging should be performed at diagnosis and considered at relapse.
For smoldering myeloma, risk models (Mayo 20/2/20) guide observation vs. early treatment.

management_principles.overview: The management of multiple myeloma (MM) and related plasma cell disorders involves a risk-adapted, multimodal approach. Treatment decisions are guided by disease stage (e.g., solitary plasmacytoma, smoldering myeloma, symptomatic MM), risk stratification (e.g., ISS, R-ISS, R2-ISS, IMS-IMWG), patient factors (age, performance status, frailty, comorbidities), and molecular characteristics (cytogenetic abnormalities, MRD status). The primary goal for symptomatic MM is to achieve deep and durable responses, while minimizing toxicity. Supportive care, including bone-modifying agents, infection prophylaxis, and management of complications (renal impairment, hypercalcemia, VTE), is integral to improving outcomes. For asymptomatic conditions like smoldering myeloma, observation or early intervention based on risk is considered. Clinical trial participation is encouraged for all eligible patients. The NCCN Panel emphasizes equitable and inclusive clinical trial design to address disparities, particularly in Black/African American individuals who have higher myeloma incidence and distinct biological features [MYEL-F].

by_intent.patient_group: Solitary plasmacytoma (osseous or extramedullary) with minimal marrow involvement (<10% clonal plasma cells)
by_intent.approach: Radiation therapy (RT) to the involved site is the intervention of choice. Surgery may be considered for structural instability or neurologic compromise. Close surveillance for progression to MM is required [MYEL-2, MYEL-D].

Risk stratification using Mayo 2018/IMWG 20/2/20 criteria. Low-risk patients may be observed at 3-6 month intervals. High-risk patients may consider clinical trials or early therapy with single-agent daratumumab (category 1) or lenalidomide (category 2B) [MYEL-3].

Primary therapy with quadruplet regimens preferred for HCT-eligible patients (e.g., Dara-VRd, Isa-VRd). For HCT-ineligible or deferred, regimens like D-Rd, Dara-VRd (for non-frail patients <80 years) are preferred. Autologous HCT is standard for eligible patients. Maintenance therapy post-HCT is recommended [MYEL-4, MYEL-5, MYEL-G].

Treatment based on prior therapies, refractory status, and depth of response. Options include triplet regimens, CAR T-cell therapy (e.g., cilta-cel, ide-cel), bispecific antibodies (e.g., elranatamab, teclistamab), or clinical trials. Consider re-challenge with prior agents if relapse after >6 months [MYEL-6, MYEL-G].

by_intent.patient_group: Refractory disease with lack of treatment options
by_intent.approach: Symptom management with palliative care specialist, supportive care, and hospice initiation as appropriate [MYEL-6].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Treatment recommendations should be made after joint consultation and/or discussion by a multidisciplinary team including hematologists/oncologists, radiation oncologists, surgeons (for plasmacytomas or fractures), radiologists, pathologists, and palliative care specialists. For complex cases like CNS disease, involvement of neurologists and neurosurgeons is advised [MYEL-D, MYEL-H].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Frailty assessment should be considered in older adults using tools like the IMWG Myeloma Frailty Score Calculator. Performance status influences regimen selection: frail patients may start with 2- or 3-drug regimens with dose modifications, and steroids should be reduced or discontinued based on response and toxicity. Renal dysfunction and advanced age are not contraindications to autologous HCT but may require adjusted dosing [MYEL-F, MYEL-G].

nodes.criteria: Biopsy-proven solitary lesion of bone or soft tissue with clonal plasma cells, normal skeletal survey/MRI of spine/pelvis, absence of myeloma-defining events, and clonal BMPCs <10%

options.label: Primary treatment
options.recommendation: RT to involved site: 40-50 Gy in 1.8-2.0 Gy fractions (20-25 fractions). For plasmacytomas <5 cm, 35-40 Gy is acceptable [MYEL-2, MYEL-D].

options.label: If structurally unstable or neurologic compromise
options.recommendation: Consider surgery for instability or mass effect [MYEL-2].

options.label: Alternative option
options.recommendation: Consider clinical trial participation [MYEL-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant systemic therapy routinely recommended. Systemic therapy may be indicated for high risk of progression based on clinical context [MYEL-2].

nodes.criteria: Low risk (0 risk factors per 20/2/20: BMPCs >20%, M-protein >2 g/dL, FLCr >20)

options.label: Active monitoring
options.recommendation: Observe at 3-6 month intervals with regular lab assessments and imaging [MYEL-3].

options.label: Alternative
options.recommendation: Enroll in clinical trial if available [MYEL-3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: No immediate therapy indicated. Close surveillance for progression to symptomatic MM.

nodes.criteria: High risk (≥2 risk factors per 20/2/20 criteria)

options.label: Preferred option
options.recommendation: Strongly recommend enrollment in ongoing clinical trial [MYEL-3].

options.label: For select patients
options.recommendation: Consider single-agent daratumumab (category 1) or lenalidomide (category 2B) based on shared decision-making [MYEL-3].

options.label: Close monitoring
options.recommendation: Observe at 3-month intervals as clinically indicated, with prompt initiation of therapy upon progression [MYEL-3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Therapy is not adjuvant but may be initiated early to delay progression. Risk criteria guide intervention.

management_pathways.title: Management of Newly Diagnosed Symptomatic Multiple Myeloma

nodes.criteria: HCT-eligible patients (all ages, no absolute contraindications)

options.label: Quadruplet regimen preferred
options.recommendation: Dara-VRd (daratumumab/bortezomib/lenalidomide/dexamethasone) or Isa-VRd (isatuximab-irfc/bortezomib/lenalidomide/dexamethasone) [category 1]. Collect stem cells within first 6 cycles [MYEL-4, MYEL-G].

options.label: Post-induction consolidation
options.recommendation: Proceed to high-dose melphalan and autologous HCT after induction. Collect sufficient stem cells for up to 2 transplants if appropriate [MYEL-5].

options.label: Post-HCT
options.recommendation: Lenalidomide maintenance [category 1]. For high-risk MM, consider doublet maintenance (e.g., carfilzomib/lenalidomide, daratumumab/lenalidomide) [MYEL-5, MYEL-G].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Induction therapy is part of primary treatment, not adjuvant. Maintenance therapy post-HCT aims to prolong remission.

nodes.criteria: HCT-ineligible or deferred (age >70, significant comorbidities, frailty, or patient choice)

options.label: Quadruplet or triplet regimen
options.recommendation: Dara-Rd (daratumumab/lenalidomide/dexamethasone) [category 1] for all. For patients <80 years who are not frail, Dara-VRd or Isa-VRd [category 1], [MYEL-4, MYEL-G].

options.label: Until progression with de-escalation as needed
options.recommendation: Continue primary therapy until progression, modifying dose/duration based on response and toxicity [MYEL-4, MYEL-G].

options.label: Consider for future HCT
options.recommendation: Strongly consider collecting and storing stem cells for potential future HCT [MYEL-G].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Primary therapy is continuous until progression. De-escalation may be considered for toxicity or response plateau.

nodes.criteria: High-risk cytogenetics [e.g., del(17p), t(4;14), t(14;16), 1q gain/amp]

options.label: Quadruplet regimen with anti-CD38 antibody
options.recommendation: Dara-VRd or Isa-VRd for HCT-eligible; Dara-VRd or Isa-VRd for HCT-ineligible if tolerated [MYEL-4, MYEL-G].

options.label: Autologous HCT
options.recommendation: Proceed to HCT after induction. Consider tandem HCT in selected cases [MYEL-5].

options.label: Doublet maintenance
options.recommendation: Consider PI/IMiD-based doublet maintenance (e.g., carfilzomib/lenalidomide, bortezomib/lenalidomide) [MYEL-G].

adjuvant_logic.summary: High-risk disease requires more aggressive induction and post-HCT maintenance to improve outcomes.

nodes.criteria: After 1-3 prior lines of therapy, lenalidomide-sensitive

options.label: Carfilzomib/lenalidomide/dexamethasone
options.recommendation: Carfilzomib/lenalidomide/dexamethasone [MYEL-G].

options.label: Daratumumab/pomalidomide/dexamethasone
options.preference: Preferred (category 1) after 1 prior therapy including lenalidomide and a PI
options.recommendation: Daratumumab/pomalidomide/dexamethasone [MYEL-G].

options.label: Ciltacabtagene autoleucel
options.preference: Preferred (category 1) after 1 prior line including IMiD and PI, and refractory to lenalidomide
options.recommendation: Ciltacabtagene autoleucel [MYEL-G].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Therapy is not adjuvant; options are based on prior exposure and refractory status.

options.label: Daratumumab/lenalidomide/dexamethasone
options.recommendation: Daratumumab/lenalidomide/dexamethasone [MYEL-G].

options.label: Isatuximab-irfc/carfilzomib/dexamethasone
options.recommendation: Isatuximab-irfc/carfilzomib/dexamethasone [MYEL-G].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Avoid bortezomib-containing regimens. Select alternative triplets based on other drug sensitivities.

nodes.criteria: After 3 prior lines, including anti-CD38 mAb, PI, and IMiD

options.label: Ciltacabtagene autoleucel or idecabtagene vicleucel
options.recommendation: Ciltacabtagene autoleucel or idecabtagene vicleucel [MYEL-G].

options.label: Elranatamab, linvoseltamab, talquetamab, or teclistamab
options.preference: Preferred (category 1) after at least 4 prior therapies
options.recommendation: Elranatamab, linvoseltamab, talquetamab, or teclistamab [MYEL-G].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Late-line therapy focuses on novel immunotherapies with distinct mechanisms.

options.label: Participation recommended
options.recommendation: Enroll in clinical trial for novel agents [MYEL-6].

options.label: Bispecific antibodies or CAR T-cell therapy
options.recommendation: Consider if eligible and available [MYEL-G].

options.label: Symptom management
options.recommendation: Refer to palliative care for symptom control and goals-of-care discussion [MYEL-6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Limited treatment options; focus on clinical trials and palliation.

management_pathways.title: Management of Multiple Myeloma with CNS Disease

nodes.criteria: Confusion, headache, visual symptoms, weakness, cranial nerve palsies; positive CSF cytology or MRI enhancement

options.label: Radiation therapy + systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Focal RT for symptomatic sites, intrathecal chemotherapy (e.g., thiotepa/hydrocortisone), and systemic therapy with CNS-penetrant agents (e.g., pomalidomide, lenalidomide, selinexor) [MYEL-H].

options.label: Symptom management
options.recommendation: Refer to palliative care specialist for symptom management [MYEL-H].

adjuvant_logic.summary: CNS disease requires aggressive local and systemic therapy. Prognosis is poor.

management_pathways.title: Management of Monoclonal Gammopathy of Renal Significance (MGRS)
branching_basis: cause of renal injury (plasma cell vs. lymphoplasmacytic clone)

nodes.criteria: Renal injury attributed to monoclonal immunoglobulin without meeting criteria for MM or WM; kidney biopsy confirms MGRS

options.label: Based on underlying clone
options.recommendation: For plasma cell-related MGRS, treat as MM (e.g., Dara-VRd). For lymphoplasmacytic-related, see NCCN Guidelines for WM/LPL [MGRS-2].

options.label: Renal protection
options.recommendation: Hydration, avoid nephrotoxins, monitor renal function [MGRS-1].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Therapy targets the pathogenic clone to prevent further renal damage.

management_pathways.title: Management of Monoclonal Immunoglobulin Deposition Disease (MIDD)

nodes.criteria: Organ damage (kidney, heart, liver) from monoclonal immunoglobulin deposits; biopsy-confirmed

options.label: Based on clone
options.recommendation: For plasma cell-related MIDD, treat as MM (e.g., Dara-VRd). For lymphoplasmacytic, see WM/LPL guidelines [MIDD-1].

options.label: Autologous HCT consolidation
options.recommendation: Consider autologous HCT for eligible patients after deep response. Solid organ transplant for affected organ after clonal response [MIDD-1].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Goal is to eliminate the pathogenic clone and preserve organ function.

management_pathways.title: Management of Monoclonal Gammopathy of Neurological Significance (MGNS)

nodes.criteria: Peripheral neuropathy with M protein, no evidence of MM or WM; high suspicion (sensory predominant, length-dependent, slow progression, demyelination by EMG/NCS) or intermediate suspicion affecting ADLs

options.label: If MYD88 mutation positive or high suspicion for WM
options.recommendation: See NCCN Guidelines for Waldenström Macroglobulinemia/Lymphoplasmacytic Lymphoma [MGNS-1].

options.label: For low suspicion or not affecting ADLs
options.recommendation: Monitor with neurology consult and repeat nerve conduction studies [MGNS-1].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Management depends on underlying etiology. Treatment is not always required.

nodes.criteria: Confirmed POEMS syndrome (mandatory criteria: polyneuropathy + monoclonal plasma cell disorder; plus one other major criterion)

options.label: For isolated bone lesions (<3 sites)
options.recommendation: RT to involved site [POEMS-2].

options.label: For eligible patients
options.recommendation: Autologous HCT as sole therapy or post-induction consolidation [POEMS-2].

options.label: For non-HCT candidates or pre-HCT
options.recommendation: Lenalidomide/dexamethasone ± daratumumab, bortezomib/dexamethasone, cyclophosphamide/dexamethasone, or pomalidomide/dexamethasone [POEMS-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment targets the clonal plasma cell disorder to improve neuropathy and other symptoms.

items.text: History and physical exam including assessment for bone pain, fractures, infections, and neurological symptoms
items.recommendation: Essential for all patients [MYEL-1].

items.text: Complete blood count (CBC) with differential and platelet count
items.recommendation: Essential for staging and monitoring [MYEL-1].

items.text: Serum chemistry: BUN/creatinine, electrolytes, liver function tests, albumin, calcium, uric acid, LDH, beta-2 microglobulin
items.recommendation: Essential for ISS staging and assessment of organ damage [MYEL-1].

items.text: Creatinine clearance (calculated or measured)
items.recommendation: Essential for renal dosing and staging [MYEL-1].

items.text: Serum quantitative immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM), SPEP, SIFE
items.recommendation: Essential for M-protein quantification and response assessment [MYEL-1].

items.text: 24-hour urine for total protein, UPEP, UIFE
items.recommendation: Essential for urinary M-protein assessment [MYEL-1].

items.text: Serum free light chain (FLC) assay
items.recommendation: Essential for FLC ratio and prognosis [MYEL-1].

items.text: N-terminal prohormone B-type natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP)/BNP
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances for cardiac assessment [MYEL-1].

items.text: Whole-body FDG-PET/CT (preferred) or low-dose CT
items.recommendation: Essential for initial diagnostic workup of bone disease and extramedullary lesions [MYEL-C].

items.text: Whole-body MRI without contrast (if PET/CT or low-dose CT negative)
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances to discern smoldering myeloma from MM [MYEL-C].

items.text: Echocardiogram
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances for suspected cardiac involvement (e.g., amyloidosis) [MYEL-1].

items.text: Tissue biopsy for suspected plasmacytoma
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances for confirmation [MYEL-1].

items.text: Unilateral bone marrow aspirate and biopsy with immunohistochemistry and/or multiparameter flow cytometry
items.recommendation: Essential for clonal plasma cell percentage and immunophenotyping [MYEL-1].

items.text: Plasma cell FISH panel (del(13), del(17p), t(4;14), t(11;14), t(14;16), t(14;20), 1q21 gain/amp, 1p deletion)
items.recommendation: Essential for risk stratification [MYEL-1].

items.text: Next-generation sequencing (NGS) for TP53 mutation
items.recommendation: Essential for high-risk assessment [MYEL-1].

items.text: Baseline clonotype identification or storage of aspirate sample for MRD testing by NGS
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances for future MRD monitoring [MYEL-1].

items.text: Serum viscosity
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances if hyperviscosity suspected [MYEL-1].

items.text: Hepatitis B and C testing, HIV screening
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances as required [MYEL-1].

items.text: Assessment for circulating plasma cells
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances as clinically indicated [MYEL-1].

items.text: Renal biopsy if albuminuria or abnormal renal function
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances to evaluate for MGRS or amyloidosis [MYEL-1].

items.text: Evaluation for light chain amyloidosis (echocardiogram, NT-proBNP, serum/urine FLC)
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances if amyloidosis suspected [MYEL-1].

items.text: Hematology/oncology
items.recommendation: Essential for treatment planning [MYEL-1].

items.text: Radiation oncology (for plasmacytomas or palliative RT)
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances [MYEL-D].

items.text: Orthopedic surgery (for fractures or spinal cord compression)
items.recommendation: Useful in certain circumstances [MYEL-I].

items.text: Palliative care
items.recommendation: Recommended for symptom management, especially in advanced disease [MYEL-6].

surgery.role: Surgery plays a limited role in MM management, primarily for diagnosis, stabilizing fractures, or relieving spinal cord compression. It is not a primary treatment for MM but may be part of multimodality approach for solitary plasmacytomas or complications.

procedures.name: Orthopedic fixation
procedures.approach: For impending or actual long-bone fractures or vertebral compression fractures
procedures.indications: Structural instability, fracture risk, or spinal cord compression [MYEL-I].
procedures.key_complications: Infection, hardware failure, delay in systemic therapy [MYEL-D].
procedures.operative_mortality: Low in elective procedures; higher in emergency settings. Not specified in document.

procedures.name: Vertebroplasty or kyphoplasty
procedures.approach: Minimally invasive procedure for vertebral compression fractures

procedures.name: Debulking surgery
procedures.approach: For solitary plasmacytomas causing neurologic compromise or mass effect
procedures.indications: Solitary plasmacytoma with spinal cord compression or neurological compromise [MYEL-2].
procedures.operative_mortality: Variable based on site and patient status; not specified in document.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Surgery is considered for structurally unstable lesions, neurologic compromise due to mass effect, or for diagnostic biopsy of suspected plasmacytoma. It is not indicated for diffuse bone disease or as primary therapy for MM [MYEL-2, MYEL-I].
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Optimize performance status, correct coagulopathy, manage infections, and consider bridging systemic therapy if needed. Ensure adequate renal function and cardiac status. For elective procedures, timing should avoid nadir of cytopenias [MYEL-D, MYEL-I].

radiation_therapy.role: RT is the primary treatment for solitary plasmacytomas and is used palliatively in MM for pain, impending fractures, or cord compression. It can be part of multimodality therapy for CNS involvement.
principles: RT is the intervention of choice for solitary plasmacytoma, providing excellent local control [MYEL-D].
For MM, RT is primarily used for palliation of symptoms like pain, cord compression, or impending fracture [MYEL-D].
Treatment planning should use modern principles: imaging-based delineation of GTV, CTV, PTV, and OARs. CTV expansions should include at least 0.5 cm margin for microscopic extent [MYEL-D].
Advanced technology (IMRT, VMAT, protons) should be used to limit doses to OARs. Principles of involved-site RT (ISRT) should be followed [MYEL-D].
Systemic therapy should not be delayed for RT; concurrent therapy can be used with careful monitoring for toxicity [MYEL-D].

dose_frameworks.name: Solitary plasmacytoma
dose_frameworks.indication: Primary treatment for solitary plasmacytoma (osseous or extramedullary) [MYEL-2, MYEL-D].

dose_frameworks.name: Solitary plasmacytoma (<5 cm)
dose_frameworks.indication: Alternative for small plasmacytomas due to high local control rates [MYEL-D].

approaches.name: Palliative RT for MM
approaches.dose_fractionation: 8 Gy x 1 fraction or 20-30 Gy in 5-15 fractions
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Systemic therapy can be given concurrently; monitor for additive toxicity [MYEL-D].
approaches.indication: Uncontrolled pain, impending pathologic fracture, or impending cord compression [MYEL-D].
approaches.key_trial: Not specified; based on panel consensus and retrospective data.
approaches.toxicities: Fatigue, myelosuppression, nausea, site-specific effects (e.g., dermatitis, esophagitis) [MYEL-D].

approaches.name: Craniospinal irradiation (CSI) for CNS disease
approaches.dose_fractionation: Variable; low-dose WBRT ≤23.4 Gy in 13 fractions may be used
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: May be combined with intrathecal chemotherapy and systemic therapy; avoid concurrent IT methotrexate and RT within 2 weeks [MYEL-H].
approaches.indication: CNS involvement with negative or controlled extra-CNS disease; as bridge to consolidation therapy [MYEL-H].
approaches.key_trial: Not specified; based on panel consensus.

approaches.name: Focal RT for CNS lesions
approaches.dose_fractionation: 30 Gy in 10 fractions or similar palliative regimen
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Systemic therapy and intrathecal chemotherapy as per multimodality approach [MYEL-H].
approaches.indication: Focal neurologic deficits from localized brain, spine, or cranial nerve disease [MYEL-H].
approaches.key_trial: Not specified; based on panel consensus.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is the mainstay for MM, including immunomodulatory drugs (IMiDs), proteasome inhibitors (PIs), monoclonal antibodies (e.g., anti-CD38), and novel agents (e.g., bispecific antibodies, CAR T-cell therapy). Regimens are categorized as preferred, other recommended, or useful in certain circumstances based on evidence and toxicity profiles. Treatment is tailored to disease stage, risk, prior therapies, and patient factors.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Daratumumab monotherapy (category 1) or lenalidomide monotherapy (category 2B) in select patients
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on AQUILA trial showing improved PFS with daratumumab vs. observation in high-risk SMM [MYEL-3].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: High-risk criteria per Mayo 2018/IMWG 20/2/20 (BMPCs >20%, M-protein >2 g/dL, FLCr >20) [MYEL-3].

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Improved PFS and MRD negativity in PERSEUS and GMMG-HD7 trials [MYEL-4, MYEL-G].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Other triplets: VRd, KRd, Dara-KRd, Isa-KRd (category 2A) [MYEL-G].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: None; all patients should be assessed for cytogenetic risk [MYEL-4].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Newly diagnosed MM, HCT-ineligible or deferred
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Dara-Rd (daratumumab/lenalidomide/dexamethasone) for all; Dara-VRd or Isa-VRd for patients <80 years who are not frail (category 1)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Improved PFS and OS in MAIA, CEPHEUS, and IMROZ trials [MYEL-4, MYEL-G].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: VRd, KRd, Rd, VCd (useful in certain circumstances) [MYEL-G].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Depends on refractory status: for lenalidomide-sensitive, KRd (category 1); for bortezomib-refractory, Dara-Rd or Isa-Kd (category 1); for anti-CD38-refractory, KPd or VMP (category 1)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on ASPIRE, POLLUX, CANDOR, IKEMA trials [MYEL-G].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Other triplets, CAR T-cell therapy (cilta-cel, ide-cel) for eligible patients [MYEL-G].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Refractory status defined by progression on or within 60 days of last therapy [MYEL-G].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: CAR T-cell therapy (cilta-cel or ide-cel) or bispecific antibodies (elranatamab, teclistamab, talquetamab, linvoseltamab) (category 1)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: High response rates in CARTITUDE-1, KarMMa-3, MagnetisMM-3, MonumenTAL-1, LINKER-MM1 trials [MYEL-G].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Refractory to anti-CD38 mAb, PI, and IMiD [MYEL-G].

drugs.name: Dexamethasone

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia (62.1% grade 3/4), thrombocytopenia (29.1% grade 3/4), infusion-related reactions, infections [MYEL-G].

drugs.name: Dexamethasone

drugs.day: 1, 2, 8, 9, 15, 16 of 28-day cycle (twice weekly) or 1, 8, 15 of 28-day cycle (once weekly)

treatment_response_assessment.title: Response Assessment for Multiple Myeloma
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response should be assessed after two consecutive assessments made any time before starting any new therapy. For MRD, no need for two consecutive assessments, but information after each treatment stage is recommended (e.g., after induction, HCT, consolidation, maintenance). [MYEL-E]
response_logic: Use IMWG uniform response criteria for all clinical trials and practice [MYEL-E].
Complete response (CR): Negative immunofixation on serum and urine, disappearance of soft tissue plasmacytomas, <5% plasma cells in bone marrow [MYEL-E].
Stringent CR (sCR): CR plus normal FLC ratio and absence of clonal cells on bone marrow biopsy by immunohistochemistry [MYEL-E].
Very good partial response (VGPR): Serum and urine M-protein detectable by immunofixation but not electrophoresis, or ≥90% reduction in serum M-protein plus urine M-protein <100 mg/24 h [MYEL-E].
Partial response (PR): ≥50% reduction of serum M-protein plus reduction in 24-h urinary M-protein by ≥90% or to <200 mg/24 h [MYEL-E].
Minimal response (MR): ≥25% but ≤49% reduction of serum M-protein and reduction in 24-h urine M-protein by 50%-89% [MYEL-E].
Stable disease (SD): Not meeting criteria for CR, VGPR, PR, MR, or PD [MYEL-E].
Progressive disease (PD): Increase of 25% from lowest confirmed response value in M-protein, appearance of new lesions, or other indicators of progression [MYEL-E].
imaging_recommendations: Same imaging modality used during initial workup should be used for follow-up assessments [MYEL-C].
Advanced whole-body imaging (FDG-PET/CT, low-dose CT, MRI without contrast) as needed. Residual focal lesions on FDG-PET/CT or MRI have adverse prognostic significance [MYEL-C].
Whole-body FDG-PET/CT recommended around day 100 after autologous HCT to assess for residual disease [MYEL-5].
For smoldering myeloma, annual imaging with same technique used at diagnosis or as clinically indicated [MYEL-C].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Bone marrow aspirate and biopsy with FISH should be performed as clinically indicated, especially at relapse to reassess cytogenetics [MYEL-4].
MRD testing by next-generation flow (NGF) or next-generation sequencing (NGS) with sensitivity of 10^-5 or higher is recommended for prognostication after shared decision with patient [MYEL-E].
If relapse occurs, consider re-biopsy to assess for clonal evolution or new cytogenetic abnormalities [MYEL-G].

prognosis.overall: Multiple myeloma (MM) is a malignant neoplasm of plasma cells that typically accumulate in bone marrow, leading to bone destruction, elevated blood calcium, and anemia as well as renal damage secondary to the secreted monoclonal protein (M-protein). MM is most frequently diagnosed among people aged 65 to 74 years, with the median age being 69 years [1]. The American Cancer Society has estimated 36,100 new MM cases and an estimated 12,030 deaths in the United States in 2025 [2]. Globally, age-standardized prevalence and mortality rates, as well as disability-adjusted life years have all increased between 1990 and 2021, with the number of MM cases, deaths, and disability-adjusted life-years higher in males than in females [3]. Prognosis varies significantly based on disease stage, cytogenetic risk, and treatment response. The Revised International Staging System (R-ISS) integrates ISS stage with cytogenetic abnormalities and LDH levels for more refined prognostication [42]. Patients achieving minimal residual disease (MRD) negativity after treatment have significantly improved PFS and OS outcomes [155].

by_stage.context: Serum beta-2 microglobulin <3.5 mg/L and serum albumin ≥3.5 g/dL
Not ISS stage I or III
ISS stage I and standard risk chromosomal abnormalities by FISH and serum LDH ≤ ULN
Not R-ISS stage I or III; intermediate risk group
ISS stage III and either high-risk chromosomal abnormalities [del(17p) or t(4;14) or t(14;16)] by FISH or serum LDH > ULN
2-year progression rate 44% per IMWG criteria [38]; median TTP 29 months with ≥2 risk factors per Mayo 20/2/20 model [37]
Five-year OS from largest retrospective study (N=258); disease-free survival 50%; local control 85% [56]

prognostic_factors: Cytogenetic abnormalities: del(1p32), t(4;14), t(14;16), t(14;20), del(17p)/monosomy 17/TP53 mutation, 1q21 gain/1q21 amplification, MYC translocation
Two or more cytogenetic abnormalities (very high risk)
Disease relapse within 2 years of initial therapy with HCT and maintenance
Relapse within 18 months in case of non-transplant-based treatment
Acquisition of 1q gain/amplification and/or del(17p)/TP53 mutation at relapse
Extramedullary disease at relapse and/or circulating plasma cells
prognosis.prognostic_context: Smoldering myeloma risk stratification uses the Mayo 2018/IMWG 20/2/20 criteria incorporating BMPCs >20%, M protein >2 g/dL, and FLCr >20. Patients with ≥2 factors have high risk with median TTP of 29 months; 0 factors yields median TTP of 110 months [37]. The IMWG further incorporates t(4;14), t(14;16), 1q gain, or del(13q) for 4-risk-group stratification with 2-year progression rates of 6%, 23%, 46%, and 63% [38]. MRD negativity by both flow cytometry and NGS at ≥10^-5 sensitivity is associated with improved PFS and OS [155,201].

disease_related.complication: Bone disease (osteolytic lesions, fractures, cord compression)
disease_related.management: All patients receiving primary myeloma therapy should be given bone-targeting treatment (bisphosphonates [category 1] or denosumab). Continue for up to 2 years. Baseline dental exam recommended. Monitor for ONJ and renal dysfunction. Orthopedic consultation for impending/actual long-bone fractures or cord compression. Palliative RT for uncontrolled pain, impending pathologic fracture, or impending cord compression using 8 Gy x 1 fraction or 20-30 Gy in 5-15 fractions [MYEL-I, MYEL-D]
Treat with hydration, bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid preferred), denosumab, steroids, and/or calcitonin [MYEL-I]
Plasmapheresis as adjunctive therapy for symptomatic hyperviscosity [MYEL-I]

Pulse dexamethasone, regimens containing bortezomib and/or daratumumab. Hydration to goal urine output 100-150 cc/h. Discontinue nephrotoxic medications. Treat hypercalcemia and hyperuricemia. Dialysis if refractory. Lenalidomide dose adjustments per renal function: CrCl ≥30 to <60 mL/min: 10 mg daily; CrCl <30 mL/min not on dialysis: 15 mg every 48h; ESRD on dialysis: 5 mg once daily [MYEL-L]

Multimodality therapy (radiation and systemic therapy) with incorporation of palliative care. RT for focal neurologic deficits. WBRT ≤23.4 Gy in 13 fractions for poorly localized disease. IT chemotherapy (Thiotepa/Hydrocortisone or Methotrexate/Cytarabine/Hydrocortisone). IMiDs with CNS penetration (pomalidomide, lenalidomide). CAR-T and BsAb therapy not precluded by treated CNS disease history [MYEL-H, CNSM-1]

VTE prophylaxis for all patients. Risk stratification by IMPEDE or SAVED score. Low risk: aspirin 81-325 mg daily. High risk: LMWH, rivaroxaban 10 mg daily, apixaban 2.5 mg BID, or warfarin (INR 2-3). Duration: indefinite while on myeloma therapy [MYEL-K]

chemotherapy: Peripheral neuropathy (bortezomib-related, especially twice-weekly dosing; subcutaneous administration preferred to reduce risk)
Neutropenia and thrombocytopenia (common with lenalidomide, pomalidomide, alkylating agents)
Increased risk of secondary malignancies especially with lenalidomide maintenance post-HCT
immunotherapy: Cytokine release syndrome (CRS) with CAR-T cell and bispecific antibody therapies
Immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS) with CAR-T and BsAb
Higher risk for CRS in Black/African American individuals receiving CAR-T therapy
Infection risk during and after CAR-T and BsAb therapies
targeted_therapy: Daratumumab and isatuximab-irfc may interfere with serologic testing causing false-positive indirect Coombs test
Monoclonal antibodies may produce false-positive serum immunofixation if monoclonal protein is IgG kappa
Carfilzomib can potentially cause cardiac, renal, and pulmonary toxicities, especially in older patients
Belantamab mafodotin-blmf: ocular events (keratopathy) notably more common than with daratumumab-based regimens
surgery: Consider surgery for structurally unstable plasmacytomas or neurologic compromise due to mass effect
Vertebroplasty or kyphoplasty for symptomatic vertebral compression fractures
Renal biopsy if albuminuria or abnormal renal function when other causes unclear
Sural nerve biopsy may be considered in certain circumstances for MGNS but associated with pain and potential permanent deficits
radiation: RT primarily used for palliation in MM
Careful planning to minimize toxicity to spinal cord, brain, bone marrow, and adjacent OARs
Systemic therapy should not be delayed for RT; can be used concurrently
Moderately fractionated courses of 20-25 Gy in 8-10 fractions preferred over higher doses to limit toxicity
Bridging RT may be considered prior to CAR-T infusion for symptomatic/at-risk disease

supportive_care.overview: Important advances have been made in adjunctive treatment/supportive care of patients with MM. This involves careful patient education about the probable side effects of each drug, the drug combinations being used, and the supportive care measures required. Supportive care can be categorized into those measures required for all patients and those that address specific drugs. Bony manifestations develop in 85% of patients with MM [MYEL-I].
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Not specifically detailed in the source document beyond general supportive care references.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Not specifically detailed in the source document. Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Supportive Care.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Hematopoietic Growth Factors for anemia management. Erythropoietin may be considered for anemic patients, especially those with renal failure [MYEL-I].
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: VTE prophylaxis is administered assuming there are no contraindications to anticoagulation agents or anti-platelets. The highest risk for VTE is in the first 6 months following new diagnosis of MM. Risk stratification uses IMPEDE or SAVED scoring systems. ≤3 points IMPEDE or <2 points SAVED: aspirin 81-325 mg daily. ≥4 points IMPEDE or ≥2 points SAVED: LMWH equivalent to enoxaparin 40 mg daily, rivaroxaban 10 mg daily, apixaban 2.5 mg BID, fondaparinux 2.5 mg daily, or warfarin (INR 2-3). Duration: indefinite while on myeloma therapy or 3-6 months followed by aspirin [MYEL-K].
supportive_care.pain_management: Palliative RT for uncontrolled bone pain. Bisphosphonates or denosumab for bone disease. Vertebroplasty or kyphoplasty for symptomatic vertebral compression fractures. Palliative care referral recommended for symptom management [MYEL-D, MYEL-I, MYEL-6].
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Palliative care specialist referral recommended for symptom management in relapsed/refractory disease and at diagnosis. Discuss patient's preferences and goals of care [MYEL-4, MYEL-6].
supportive_care.dental_care: A baseline dental exam is strongly recommended before starting bone-targeting treatment (bisphosphonates or denosumab). Monitor for osteonecrosis of the jaw (ONJ). Risk of ONJ is 9.5-fold greater with zoledronic acid compared to pamidronate [334]. Long-term ONJ rates higher with zoledronic acid (3.7%) versus clodronate (0.5%) per MRC Myeloma IX [337], [MYEL-I].

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: After primary therapy, patients should undergo response assessment using IMWG criteria. For HCT-eligible patients, autologous HCT is preferred after primary therapy with category 1 evidence. Whole-body FDG-PET/CT is recommended around day 100 after autologous HCT [MYEL-5]. MRD testing should be considered for prognostication after shared decision with patient. Follow-up with advanced whole-body imaging (FDG-PET/CT, low-dose CT, MRI without contrast) annually or as clinically indicated [MYEL-4, MYEL-5].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Residual focal lesions detected by either FDG-PET/CT or MRI have been shown to be of adverse prognostic significance [148-151]. Zamagni et al reported PFS of 44 months in patients with residual focal lesions on FDG-PET/CT versus 84 months for those without residual focal lesions [150]. In the IMAJEM trial, both PFS and OS were significantly better in patients with negative FDG-PET/CT results before maintenance therapy [151]. MRD negativity predicts improved PFS and OS, including in those who achieved CR [155].
late_effects_screening: Monitor for osteonecrosis of the jaw with bisphosphonate/denosumab use
Monitor for peripheral neuropathy (especially with bortezomib-based regimens)
Screen for secondary primary malignancies, especially with lenalidomide maintenance post-HCT
Monitor for cardiac, renal, and pulmonary toxicity with carfilzomib
Monitor for ocular toxicity with belantamab mafodotin-blmf
Assess for infection risk and immune reconstitution, especially after CAR-T and BsAb therapy
Renal function monitoring with bisphosphonate therapy
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Disease relapse within 2 years of initial therapy with HCT and maintenance is considered high risk. Relapse within 18 months of primary induction therapy without HCT is also high risk. Solitary plasmacytoma has relatively high risk of progression into MM (14%-38% within the first 3 years of diagnosis) [49]. Patients may evolve from low-risk to high-risk smoldering myeloma over time.

clinical_follow_up_schedule.schedule: Every 3-6 months for at least 5 years. All plasmacytomas should be imaged yearly, preferably with the same technique used at diagnosis, for at least 5 years [MYEL-2]
Every 3-6 months. Patients with rising parameters are considered high risk and should be closely monitored [MYEL-3]

Laboratory assessments for monitoring treatment toxicities: CBC with differential and metabolic panel. Serum quantitative immunoglobulins, SPEP, SIFE. 24-hour urine for total protein, UPEP, UIFE as clinically indicated. Serum FLC assay. Bone marrow aspirate and biopsy at relapse with FISH as clinically indicated. Consider MRD testing as indicated [MYEL-4]

imaging_strategy.recommendation: Whole-body FDG-PET/CT (preferred) or whole-body low-dose CT is recommended. Skeletal survey acceptable in certain circumstances [MYEL-C]
Consider whole-body MRI without contrast to discern smoldering myeloma from MM [MYEL-C]
Whole-body MRI (or FDG-PET/CT if MRI not available) is first choice for initial evaluation [MYEL-C]
Whole-body FDG-PET/CT is first choice for initial and continued evaluation [MYEL-C]
Advanced whole-body imaging (FDG-PET/CT, low-dose CT, MRI without contrast) annually or as clinically indicated, ideally same technique used at diagnosis [MYEL-C]
Advanced whole-body imaging (FDG-PET/CT, low-dose CT, MRI without contrast) as needed. Patients without measurable M protein or FLC should be followed using imaging at regular intervals [MYEL-C]
Whole-body FDG-PET/CT recommended around day 100 after autologous HCT [MYEL-5]

laboratory_monitoring: CBC with differential and platelet count
Serum chemistry: creatinine, electrolytes, liver function tests, calcium, albumin, uric acid, LDH, beta-2 microglobulin
Serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP) and serum immunofixation electrophoresis (SIFE)
24-hour urine for total protein, urine protein electrophoresis (UPEP), and urine immunofixation electrophoresis (UIFE)
Bone marrow aspirate and biopsy with FISH, NGS, or multi-parameter flow cytometry as clinically indicated
MRD testing by NGF or NGS when indicated
supportive_follow_up: Monitor for ONJ with bisphosphonate/denosumab use
Monitor renal function with bisphosphonate therapy
VTE risk assessment and prophylaxis while on myeloma therapy
Assess for peripheral neuropathy at each visit
Consider immunoglobulin replacement for IgG <400 mg/dL or recurrent life-threatening infections

special_situations.key_message: Frailty assessment should be considered in older adults. The IMWG Myeloma Frailty Score Calculator is recommended for prognosis assessment.
special_situations.management: Consider dose modifications based on functional status and age. Consider discontinuing dexamethasone at earliest possible timepoint. Steroids reduced to 20 mg weekly in older patients and decreased/discontinued with response plateau and/or toxicity. VRD-lite regimen available for frail patients [MYEL-F].

special_situations.key_message: Black/African American individuals have the highest rate of myeloma of any race/ethnic group and twice the incidence of all other races and ethnicities. They present with more severe symptoms at diagnosis including increased anemia, higher calcium levels, worse renal dysfunction, and more extramedullary disease.
special_situations.management: Biological differences: more likely to have chromosome 14 translocations (t(11;14), t(14;16), t(14;20)) and less likely to have high-risk mutations (del17p, gain/amp 1q). Superior median OS with equal treatment access (7.07 years vs 5.83 years for white patients; P < .001) [69,70]. Toxicity differences: hyperpigmentation with IMiDs, higher CRS risk with CAR-T. Duffy-null phenotype in ~67% of healthy Black/African American individuals should not lead to inappropriate dose reductions. Equitable and inclusive clinical trials recommended.

special_situations.key_message: CNS involvement may occur as part of initial presentation or relapse. Hyperammonemic encephalopathy is not considered CNS involvement. Extension of skull/spinal plasmacytoma without meningeal infiltration is not considered CNS disease.
special_situations.management: Multimodality therapy with RT, systemic therapy, and palliative care. RT for focal deficits; WBRT ≤23.4 Gy for poorly localized disease. IT chemotherapy. IMiDs (pomalidomide, lenalidomide) have clinical CNS penetration. CAR-T and BsAb not precluded by treated CNS disease. Proteasome inhibitors have limited BBB penetration with no clear CNS activity [CNSM-1, MYEL-H].

special_situations.key_message: Risk of progression to MM is 14%-38% within first 3 years. Osseous plasmacytoma has higher 2-year progression risk (35%) compared to extramedullary lesions (7%) [48].
special_situations.management: RT is intervention of choice. RT 40-50 Gy in 1.8-2.0 Gy fractions (20-25 fractions) to involved site; 35-40 Gy acceptable for <5 cm tumors. Consider surgery if structurally unstable or neurologic compromise. FISH testing on plasmacytoma biopsy if clonal cells absent/inadequate in marrow. Annual imaging for at least 5 years [MYEL-2, MYEL-D].

special_situations.key_message: MGRS describes patients meeting MGUS criteria with renal injury attributable to M protein. Renal damage in setting of symptomatic MM is not considered MGRS. Kidney biopsy is essential to demonstrate nephrotoxicity.
special_situations.management: Treatment directed at underlying plasma cell or B-cell clones. For IgG/IgA/FLC MGRS: use MM management algorithms. For IgM MGRS: see WM/LPL guidelines. For MGRS with MBL features: see CLL/SLL guidelines. Response assessment includes SPEP/SIFE, 24-hour urine, FLC, and serum creatinine [MGRS-1, MGRS-2].

special_situations.key_message: Rare plasma cell disorder with demyelinating peripheral neuropathy, clonal plasma cell proliferation, and VEGF elevation. Diagnosis requires both mandatory major criteria (polyneuropathy + monoclonal plasma cell disorder, almost always λ), one of 3 other major criteria (Castleman disease, sclerotic bone lesions, VEGF elevation), and one of 6 minor criteria.
special_situations.management: RT alone for isolated bone lesions (<3 sites) without clonal BMPCs. Autologous HCT for eligible patients. Induction options: Rd ± daratumumab, bortezomib/dexamethasone, cyclophosphamide/dexamethasone, pomalidomide/dexamethasone. Melphalan/dexamethasone for non-HCT candidates. Bortezomib may exacerbate neuropathy [POEMS-1 through POEMS-4].

special_situations.key_message: Renal disease seen in 20%-50% of patients with MM [349-351]. Usually due to light chain cast nephropathy but other etiologies must be considered including amyloidosis and MIDD.
special_situations.management: Bortezomib and/or daratumumab-containing regimens can be administered in severe renal impairment and dialysis without dose adjustment [352-356]. Hydration to goal urine output 100-150 cc/h. Discontinue nephrotoxic medications. Mechanical removal of FLCs with high cutoff dialysis or plasmapheresis may have limited role. Renal dosing of lenalidomide: CrCl 30-60: 10 mg daily; CrCl <30 not on dialysis: 15 mg q48h; ESRD on dialysis: 5 mg daily post-dialysis [MYEL-L].

clinical_pearls: Quadruplet regimens are now preferred as standard for primary treatment of all HCT-eligible patients with MM based on improved response rates, depth of response, and PFS/OS seen in clinical trials [MYEL-G].

The 20/2/20 risk model (BMPCs >20%, M protein >2 g/dL, FLCr >20) is essential for risk stratifying smoldering myeloma; patients with ≥2 factors have high risk with median TTP of 29 months [37, MYEL-3].
Daratumumab and isatuximab-irfc may cause false-positive indirect Coombs test and interfere with serologic testing; type and screen must be performed before using these agents [MYEL-F].
Subcutaneous bortezomib is the preferred route of administration based on MMY-3021 trial data showing noninferior efficacy with significantly reduced peripheral neuropathy [78, MYEL-F].
Black/African American individuals have twice the incidence of MM, more severe symptoms at diagnosis, but superior OS with equal treatment access (7.07 vs 5.83 years; P < .001) [69,70].

Talquetamab-tgvs may be considered as a bridge to BCMA CAR-T therapy in relapsed/refractory MM, but BCMA-targeted CAR-T immediately following BCMA-targeted bispecific use may not represent optimal sequencing [MYEL-G 5 of 5].
The IMWG response criteria should be used in all clinical trials and clinical practice; MRD negativity (by NGF and/or NGS at ≥10^-5 sensitivity) predicts improved PFS and OS [147,155].

For bone disease management, continue bisphosphonates or denosumab for up to 2 years with frequency based on individual patient criteria and response; continuing beyond 2 years based on clinical judgment [MYEL-I].

key_trials.full_name: Daratumumab, bortezomib, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone for Multiple Myeloma
key_trials.intervention: Dara-VRd induction (4 cycles) → HCT → 2 cycles consolidation → Dara-R maintenance
key_trials.comparator: VRd induction → HCT → 2 cycles consolidation → R maintenance
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS not reached in either arm at 47.5 months; PFS rates 84.3% vs 67.7% (HR, 0.42; 95% CI, 0.30-0.59; P < .001)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: CR or better: 87.9% vs 70.1% (P < .001); MRD negativity by NGS <10^-5: 75.2% vs 47.5% (P < .001)
key_trials.practice_change: Established Dara-VRd as category 1 preferred regimen for HCT-eligible NDMM

key_trials.full_name: Addition of daratumumab to lenalidomide, bortezomib, and dexamethasone for transplantation-eligible patients with NDMM
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Deep and durable responses with high rates of sCR and MRD negativity
key_trials.practice_change: Supports clinical benefit of adding daratumumab to VRd in HCT-eligible setting

key_trials.full_name: Bortezomib with lenalidomide and dexamethasone versus lenalidomide and dexamethasone alone in patients with previously untreated myeloma
key_trials.population: Previously untreated MM without intent for immediate HCT
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Longer-term follow-up (84 months): PFS 41 vs 29 months; OS not yet reached vs 69 months
key_trials.practice_change: Established VRd as category 1 regimen for HCT candidates

key_trials.full_name: Daratumumab, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone versus lenalidomide and dexamethasone alone in NDMM (HCT-ineligible)
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS not reached vs 34.4 months (HR, 0.68; P < .0001); Updated (64.5 months): PFS 61.9 vs 34.4 months (HR, 0.55; P < .0001); OS not reached vs 65.5 months (HR, 0.66; P = .0003)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ≥CR 51.1% vs 30.1%; MRD negativity 32.1% vs 11.1%; Sustained MRD negativity ≥18 months: 16.8% vs 3.3%
key_trials.practice_change: Established Dara-Rd as category 1 preferred regimen for HCT-ineligible NDMM

key_trials.full_name: Daratumumab plus bortezomib, lenalidomide and dexamethasone for transplant-ineligible or transplant-deferred NDMM
key_trials.population: HCT-ineligible or deferred NDMM patients <80 years, not frail
key_trials.key_result: MRD negativity 60.9% vs 39.4%; PFS HR 0.57 (95% CI, 0.41-0.79; P = .0005); Median PFS not reached vs 52.6 months
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: 54-month PFS estimates: 68.1% vs 49.5%
key_trials.practice_change: Established Dara-VRd as category 1 for patients <80 years who are not frail

key_trials.full_name: Isatuximab-irfc, bortezomib, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone for MM
key_trials.population: NDMM ≤80 years, not eligible for ASCT
key_trials.practice_change: Established Isa-VRd as category 1 preferred for HCT-deferred patients <80 years not frail

key_trials.full_name: Carfilzomib or bortezomib in combination with lenalidomide and dexamethasone for NDMM without intention for immediate HCT
key_trials.population: NDMM without high-risk features (except t(4;14)), not intending immediate HCT
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS 34.4 months (VRd) vs 34.6 months (KRd); similar PFS with less neuropathy but more cardiac/pulmonary/renal toxicity with KRd
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: VGPR or better 65% vs 74% (P = .0015)
key_trials.practice_change: KRd listed as other recommended but not preferred due to similar efficacy with different toxicity profile

key_trials.full_name: Lenalidomide, bortezomib, and dexamethasone with transplantation for myeloma
key_trials.key_result: PFS 50 vs 36 months with HCT; CR 59% vs 48% (P = .03); MRD-negative 79% vs 65% (P < .001)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: OS high in both groups; HCT improved PFS but not OS
key_trials.practice_change: Demonstrates benefit of autologous HCT with higher rates of durable responses

key_trials.full_name: Triplet therapy, transplantation, and maintenance until progression in myeloma
key_trials.intervention: 3 cycles VRd → stem cell collection → HCT → 2 cycles VRd → R maintenance
key_trials.comparator: 3 cycles VRd → stem cell collection → 5 cycles VRd → R maintenance
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS improved by 21 months with HCT at 76-month follow-up, but no OS benefit; 5-year survival 79.2% vs 80.7% (HR for death, 1.10; 95% CI, 0.73-1.65)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: MRD-negative patients had similar PFS regardless of HCT (5-year PFS 59% vs 54%; HR, 0.91)
key_trials.practice_change: Suggests delaying HCT is an option without negative OS effects

key_trials.full_name: Ciltacabtagene autoleucel versus standard care in lenalidomide-refractory MM
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS not reached vs 11.8 months (HR, 0.26; 95% CI, 0.18-0.38; P < .001); CR or better 73.1% vs 21.8%; Updated: 30-month OS 76.4% vs 63.8% (HR, 0.55; P = .0009)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR 84.6% vs 67.3%; MRD negativity 60.6% vs 15.6%
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 preferred CAR-T for after one prior line including IMiD and PI, refractory to lenalidomide

key_trials.full_name: Idecabtagene vicleucel versus standard regimens in relapsed and refractory MM
key_trials.population: RRMM after 2-4 prior lines, including IMiD, anti-CD38, and PI
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Deep responses in heavily pretreated population (95% daratumumab-refractory)
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 preferred CAR-T for after two prior lines including IMiD, anti-CD38, and PI

key_trials.full_name: Carfilzomib, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone for relapsed multiple myeloma
key_trials.population: Relapsed/refractory MM with 1-3 prior therapies, lenalidomide-sensitive
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Superior health-related quality of life with KRd
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 preferred for relapsed/refractory MM

key_trials.full_name: Carfilzomib, dexamethasone, and daratumumab versus carfilzomib and dexamethasone for relapsed/refractory MM
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Deeper responses with daratumumab addition
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 preferred for relapsed/refractory MM

key_trials.full_name: Isatuximab, carfilzomib, and dexamethasone in relapsed MM
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade ≥3 TRAE 77% in Isa group vs 67% in control
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 preferred for relapsed/refractory MM

key_trials.full_name: Daratumumab plus pomalidomide and dexamethasone versus pomalidomide and dexamethasone alone in previously treated MM
key_trials.key_result: PFS 12.4 vs 6.9 months (P = .0018)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Serious adverse events 50% vs 39%
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 preferred for relapsed/refractory MM after one prior line including IMiD and PI

key_trials.full_name: Belantamab mafodotin for relapsed or refractory MM
key_trials.key_result: ORR 32% and 35% in 2.5 and 3.4 mg/kg cohorts; median PFS 2.8 and 3.9 months
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median OS 15.3 and 14.0 months; Grade 3-4 keratopathy 29% and 25%
key_trials.practice_change: Useful in certain circumstances for after 4 prior therapies

key_trials.full_name: Belantamab mafodotin plus bortezomib and dexamethasone versus daratumumab plus bortezomib and dexamethasone
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: MRD negativity in ≥CR: 25% vs 10% (P < .0001); Ocular events 79% vs 29%
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 other recommended after ≥2 prior therapies including IMiD and PI

key_trials.full_name: Daratumumab or active monitoring for high-risk smoldering multiple myeloma
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: PFS benefit clear in high-risk SMM but not intermediate or low risk
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 for high-risk smoldering myeloma: single-agent daratumumab

key_trials.full_name: Once-per-week selinexor, bortezomib, and dexamethasone versus twice-per-week bortezomib and dexamethasone
key_trials.population: Previously treated MM with 1-3 prior lines including PIs
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade 3-4 thrombocytopenia 39% vs 17%
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 other recommended for previously treated MM

key_trials.full_name: Elranatamab in relapsed or refractory MM
key_trials.key_result: ORR 61.0%; ≥CR 35.0%; 80% maintained/improved response ≥6 months with biweekly dosing
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade 3-4 AEs decreased from 58.6% to 46.6% with biweekly dosing
key_trials.practice_change: Preferred option for after ≥4 prior lines of therapy

key_trials.full_name: Ciltacabtagene autoleucel in relapsed or refractory MM
key_trials.population: RRMM with ≥3 prior lines including IMiD, PI, and anti-CD38
key_trials.key_result: ORR 97%; sCR 67%; PFS 77%; OS 89%. Post-hoc analysis at 61.3 months: 33% progression-free without additional MM treatment, 96.9% achieved sCR
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: CRS in 95%; Grade 3-4 neutropenia 95%; anemia 68%; thrombocytopenia 60%
key_trials.practice_change: Preferred CAR-T option for after ≥4 prior lines

key_trials.full_name: Talquetamab for relapsed/refractory MM
key_trials.key_result: T-cell redirecting bispecific antibody targeting GPRC5D and CD3
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: CRS (77-80%); skin-related events (67-70%); dysgeusia (63-57%)
key_trials.practice_change: Preferred option for after ≥4 prior lines of therapy

key_trials.full_name: Linvoseltamab for relapsed/refractory MM
key_trials.population: RRMM after ≥3 lines or triplet therapy
key_trials.key_result: ORR 71%; 50% achieved ≥CR; estimated median DoR 29.4 months
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade 3-4 hematologic/infectious toxicities 74%; CRS 46%; ICANS 7.7%
key_trials.practice_change: Preferred option for after ≥4 prior lines of therapy

key_trials.full_name: Teclistamab in relapsed or refractory MM
key_trials.population: Triple class refractory MM with median 5 prior lines
key_trials.key_result: ORR 63%; ≥CR 39.4%; median PFS 11.3 months; median DoR 18.4 months
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: CRS 72.1% (grade 3: 0.6%); Grade 3-4 neutropenia 64.2%; infections grade 3-4 44.8%
key_trials.practice_change: Preferred option for after ≥4 prior lines of therapy

key_trials.full_name: Talquetamab plus Teclistamab in relapsed/refractory MM
key_trials.key_result: ORR 80% at recommended phase 2 dose (0.8 mg/kg talquetamab + 3.0 mg/kg teclistamab); 86% probability of maintaining response after 18 months
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Tolerable with prophylactic tocilizumab consideration for CRS reduction
key_trials.practice_change: Useful in certain circumstances option; prophylactic tocilizumab may be considered

key_trials.full_name: Oral ixazomib, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone for MM
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: High-risk cytogenetics subgroup showed similar HR
key_trials.practice_change: Category 1 preferred for previously treated MM after 1-3 prior therapies
